main_sections.title: Kalakritiyon ka Bharat
main_sections.prose_summary: This section opens with a short introductory passage, 'Hamara Bharat — Incredible India!', which describes Bharat as a land celebrated for its wise and heroic people, diverse culture, rich geography (rivers, lakes, forests, mountains), and unity in diversity. A brief 'Let us discuss' block invites students to reflect on Bharat's identity before moving into the main informational text. The main text, 'Kalakritiyon ka Bharat', is framed as an Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat video conference in which four children from different Indian states each share a traditional art form from their region. Aakansha from Uttarakhand describes Aipan, a folk art of geometric and nature-inspired designs drawn with white rice flour paste on brick-red (geru) walls and floors during family functions and festivals — an exclusively female art form. Priyaranjan from Odisha introduces Dhokra, a 4,000-year-old tribal metal craft using the lost-wax (cire perdue) process: a clay figure is coated in wax, fine details are carved, then covered in clay and fired; the melted wax is replaced by molten brass scrap, which takes the wax's shape after the outer clay layer is removed. Chitra from Kerala describes coconut shell craft, a biodegradable process of cleaning, smoothing, shaping, stabilising, and polishing brown coconut shells into household items such as bowls and jewellery. Balamurali from Andhra Pradesh explains the 400-year-old Kondapalli toy-making tradition: soft local wood is carved in parts, joined with makku (tamarind seed powder and sawdust paste), detailed, and coloured with oil, water-colours, vegetable dyes, or enamel paints. Themes depicted include folk stories, animals, birds, bullock carts, and rural life. A closing informational note describes the Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat programme as a government initiative to promote unity in diversity through state-pairing, mutual cultural exchange, and nation-building.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us read the following passage. [Hamara Bharat — Incredible India!] Let us discuss: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What has Bharat always been known for?
What is attractive about Bharat?
What is special about the fact that the people live here in unison?
What geographical features does the passage mention?
What is the advice given to everyone?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. Our country is beautiful and every state has its own uniqueness. The words given below are part of our identity. Complete these words with the correct vowels (A, E, I, O, U).

question_bank.stem: We like to decorate our homes with our f __ l k __ r t. (folkart)
Our c __ l t __ r e consists of our ideas, customs, traditions, values, arts, and languages. (culture)
All of us celebrate our f __ s t __ v __ l s together. (festivals)
We tell stories to our children to teach them about our c __ l t __ r e, h __ s t __ r y, and l __ g __ n d s. (culture, history, legends)
We have our own l __ n g __ a g __ s to talk to each other. (languages)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us read — Part I. Let us discuss: 1. What is the Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat programme all about? 2. Why is this programme being conducted?

question_bank.stem: What is the Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat programme all about?
Why is this programme being conducted?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss — On the basis of what the speakers said about the art forms, complete the table given below.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table: Name of the child | State | Art form | Things needed. (Akanksha — Aipan; Priyaranjan — Dhokra — clay, brass scrap, wax, fire; Chitra — Kerala — coconut shell craft; Balamurali — Andhra Pradesh — Kondapalli)
question_bank.table:
Name of the child|State|Art form|Things needed
Akanksha|Uttarakhand|Aipan|white rice flour paste, geru
Priyaranjan|Odisha|Dhokra|clay, brass scrap, wax, fire
Chitra|Kerala|Coconut shell craft|coconut shell, sandpaper, wood polish
Balamurali|Andhra Pradesh|Kondapalli|soft wood, makku, colours/dyes

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — I. Fill in the blanks to complete the following sentences.

question_bank.stem: The designs for Aipan are taken from ________________ and ________________.
Dhokra metal craft is very old because it is ________________ old.
A base is needed for coconut shell craft to make it ________________.
Soft wood, ________________ and colours are used to make Kondapalli toys.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect — II, III, IV. Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Why does Akanksha's family make Aipan?
In Dhokra, why does wax come out of the small openings?
What is common in the toys made in Balamurali's village and coconut shell craft in Kerala?

listening.transcript_text: Namaste! You must be tired and hungry after listening to so many people. I am Sushil from Jabalpur in Madhya Pradesh. Let me tell you a unique dish of my district. It is called gakkad bharta. First, we make gakkad from wheat dough. Then, we make balls with that dough and roast them on high heat. To make bharta, we roast brinjals and tomatoes. Then we garnish the bharta with chopped onions, coriander, green chillis and salt. This food item is made without frying. To eat it, you simply crumble the gakkad and drizzle it with some bharta and a spoonful of ghee. Come to Jabalpur and try it!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Listen to the description of gakkad bharta and complete the notes given below. (refer to page 162 for transcript)

question_bank.stem: The town of the speaker : __________________________
The state he belongs to : __________________________
The name of the food item : __________________________
Vegetables roasted for bharta : __________________________

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, listen to the description again and complete the flowchart on how to make gakkad bharta.

question_bank.stem: Step 1: To make gakkad, make balls of ___________ dough and ___________ them on high heat.
Step 2: To make bharta, ___________ brinjal and tomatoes and garnish with ___________ onions, coriander, green chillies and salt.
Step 3: To serve, crumble the gakkad, put some ___________ on it and add one spoon of ghee.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Speak about a local art form of your region. It can be any one out of painting, rangoli, metal craft, embroidery, music, dance or drama. Remember to include: the name of the art form, what things do they need for it, how do they make/present it. You may use the words given below when you speak: I know about the art form called … / They need ... / To make it, first ... after that ... next ... then ... at last ...

question_bank.stem: Speak about a local art form of your region (painting, rangoli, metal craft, embroidery, music, dance, or drama). Include the name, what is needed, and how it is made/presented.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You heard children from different states speak about the art forms from their state. Discuss in pairs which art form you liked the most. Now, write a paragraph with five sentences giving your reasons for liking it.

question_bank.stem: Write a paragraph with five sentences giving your reasons for liking the art form you liked most from what the children described.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Priyaranjan, Chitra and Balamurali share how art works of their states are made. They use some words to show the order of making it. Those words are: to begin, first, then, after that, next, finally, at last. Now, use some of these words to complete the paragraph given below. Use one word only once. — 'How I Get Ready for School'

question_bank.stem: 1. ____________ I get up and go to take a shower. 2. ____________, I wear my school dress. 3. ____________, I eat my food. 4. ____________, I wear my shoes. 5. ____________, I pick up my bag and go out of my home.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The children from different states each speak about one art form from their state. When they speak, they use the first form of the verb (present tense). With he, she and it, -s/-es is used with the verb. Make five sentences with the help of the words given in the table below. Use -s/-es wherever necessary. Two examples are given.

question_bank.stem: Make five complete sentences by combining pronouns (I/You/He/She/It/We/They), verbs (ask/know/tell/draw/make/practise/speak), and sentence parts (how to dance / questions to understand / beautiful flowers / a story every day / very loudly / paintings in free periods / spellings on Friday). Use -s/-es where needed.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Madhubani painting is a traditional art form from Bihar. The artists use natural colours to make them on the walls of their houses. You can also make and use natural colours as given below: yellow from haldi, green from leaves, red from red flowers/geru/sindoor, orange from flowers, blue from neel/indigo powder. Given below is a Madhubani painting. Colour it with natural colours or any other colour.

question_bank.stem: Prepare natural colours using the given materials (haldi, leaves, geru, flowers, indigo powder). Use them to colour the Madhubani painting provided.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read about the Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat programme (p.140). The programme aims to enhance interaction and promote mutual understanding between people of different states/UTs through state/UT pairing.

question_bank.stem: Find out which state your state is paired with under the Ek Bharat, Shreshtha Bharat programme. What cultural practices from that state would you like to learn about?
